caveats: a: No six-digit occupation mix or task-time study was found for the narrowed blood and plasma lens.

a: FDA device clearances establish existing technical capabilities but not adoption or labor hours saved at lower-middle-market centers.

rho: Existing automation is substantial, so gross technology capability can overstate incremental opportunity.
rho: FDA validation, traceability, quality, and cybersecurity requirements slow deployment and preserve accountable human review.

q: Capacity gains have little value when donor recruitment, product mix, shelf life, or contracted demand is the binding constraint.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Operators receive per-unit product or processing revenue, contracted hospital or manufacturer payments, collection and testing fees, and sometimes donor-related service revenue; customers require validated collection, traceability, quality, storage, and delivery rather than software alone.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 621991 combines blood and plasma operations with organ procurement and reproductive or tissue banking, whose regulation, ownership, workflows, and revenue models are too different for one coherent screen. The lens is narrowed to recurring independent blood and source-plasma collection, processing, storage, and distribution operations, without selecting among them for attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Organ procurement organizations, transplant programs, sperm, egg, embryo, placenta, and eye or tissue banks; captive hospital transfusion services; facilities owned inside national blood-center or plasma chains; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent operators providing recurring blood or source-plasma donor recruitment, collection, processing, testing coordination, storage, inventory management, and distribution services to hospitals or plasma-product manufacturers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations only for broader NAICS 621900, which includes ambulances and miscellaneous ambulatory services, while O*NET describes phlebotomy across employers and FDA describes functions already handled by blood-establishment software and automated separators. I map scheduling, donor outreach, questionnaire review, routine eligibility flags, data entry, label and record checks, inventory allocation, forecasting, exception triage, and reporting to incremental AI exposure, while retaining venipuncture, donor monitoring, equipment handling, laboratory judgment, quality release, and physical cold-chain work. The broader BLS mix can bias either way; crediting already deployed donor-management, laboratory-interface, and apheresis automation as new opportunity would bias the estimate upward.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix or task-time study was found for the narrowed blood and plasma lens.
The BLS occupation table includes ambulance services and other miscellaneous ambulatory care, while O*NET spans all phlebotomy employers.
FDA device clearances establish existing technical capabilities but not adoption or labor hours saved at lower-middle-market centers.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted staffing table plus time-and-motion studies from independent blood and plasma centers separating donor contact, phlebotomy, testing, records, quality release, inventory, logistics, and already-automated machine cycles would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Blood and plasma operations contain high-volume standardized information and logistics tasks, but their central collection and safety work remains physical and regulated. O*NET shows phlebotomists both enter and track information and perform venipuncture, donor assessment, monitoring, specimen handling, and equipment maintenance. FDA-cleared systems already cover screening, laboratory results, barcodes, quality, inventory, shipping, and product release, limiting the share that is genuinely not yet automated.

d5.bridge: AABB's national survey measures physical blood-component collections, distributions, and transfusions, while BLS projects constant-dollar output for broader NAICS 621900. I treat the recent 1% to 5% blood-supply variation and mixed component trends as a near-flat central quantity anchor, then allow modest growth from platelet and apheresis demand and broader ambulatory output, and downside from patient blood management, less-invasive surgery, donor shortages, and plasma or RBC declines. The BLS population likely overstates growth for blood services, while excluding source-plasma and biotherapy growth from the AABB transfusion survey could understate it.
caveats: NBCUS blood-component measures do not fully cover source plasma for further manufacture or all services in the narrowed lens.
The BLS projection is four-digit and includes ambulance and miscellaneous ambulatory services.
Component trends diverge, and changes in clinical practice can reduce units while raising processing complexity.
d5.change_evidence: Repeated NBCUS and FDA source-plasma series through 2026, separated by component, collection channel, units, real service revenue, patient blood-management adoption, and independent-center share, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The 2023 NBCUS reported 11.58 million RBC units collected and 10.32 million transfused, both down, while platelet distributions and transfusions increased and plasma distributions and transfusions declined. AABB described recent cycles as a stabilized baseline. This supports approximately flat physical demand with meaningful component-mix uncertainty rather than direct use of the broader 2.9% annual output projection.

caveats: No source measures eligibility in the specified earnings band or narrowed blood and plasma lens.
NAICS 621991 includes nonprofit and public-interest entities as well as commercial plasma operations and reproductive tissue banks.
The frozen firm count may treat legally distinct facilities or entities differently from economically independent control targets.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census matching FDA blood-establishment registrations to ultimate ownership, NAICS classification, normalized EBITDA, nonprofit status, customer concentration, license transferability, management depth, and independent versus chain ownership would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The official industry includes a much broader mix than the narrowed lens. Eligibility is reduced by nonprofit blood systems, organ procurement organizations, reproductive and tissue banks, captive hospital units, facilities owned by large national chains, highly concentrated customer relationships, licenses tied to facilities or processes, and operations lacking independent management. Independent regional blood or plasma operators with recurring hospital or manufacturer demand, transferable registrations and contracts, compliant sites, and management depth remain eligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621991 ($5,586,877,704) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621991 ($20,233,246,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $232,617 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621991); class <5: 110 firms, avg revenue/firm $699,491 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $110,520 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,004,972 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $158,786 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,787,477 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $440,421 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,349,516 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,793,224 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 69 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,057,246 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,751,045 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $418,654,053 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $66,147,340 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 131

o.bridge: Census defines the current industry around physical collection, storage, and distribution, O*NET documents hands-on donor work, and FDA requires traceable regulated steps and trained clinical interpretation. I translate these requirements into a high share of year-five quantity that still needs a blood or plasma operator, while allowing some elimination through hospital consolidation, customer insourcing, centralized testing, self-service donor intake, patient blood management, or direct manufacturer ownership. The sources establish operator functions rather than measuring substitution, so centralized industry consolidation could make the estimate too high.
caveats: No source directly measures year-five operator-required share for independent lower-middle-market firms.
Hospital systems and plasma manufacturers can vertically integrate or consolidate collection and processing.
Software substitution is limited, but organizational substitution through larger networks may be material.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal channel study tracking blood and plasma volume retained by independent operators versus insourced by hospitals or manufacturers, centralized into large systems, eliminated through patient blood management, or replaced by self-service technology would materially change the value.
o.rationale: Software can automate communication, records, inventory, and parts of decision support, but cannot independently recruit and monitor donors, collect blood, operate collection equipment, maintain a cold chain, release regulated product, and distribute physical components. FDA also requires traceability for each significant step and reserves final clinical interpretation for trained users in its software example.

caveats: No public source was found for billing mix or retained automation benefit in eligible independent blood and plasma operations.
Product revenue, service fees, donor economics, and testing or logistics pass-through can coexist in the same contract.
Capacity gains have little value when donor recruitment, product mix, shelf life, or contracted demand is the binding constraint.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from independent centers showing unit pricing, hospital and manufacturer contract terms, donor compensation, testing and logistics pass-through, renewal repricing, automation capital and software expense, utilization, discard rates, and realized contribution-margin changes would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Per-unit and contracted service pricing can allow an operator to retain labor, discard, inventory, and throughput improvements until hospital or manufacturer contracts reset. Retention is reduced by customer purchasing power, reimbursement pressure, donor compensation, regulated testing and quality costs, software and equipment expense, required redundancy, gain-sharing, contract rebids, and the limited cash value of faster throughput when donor supply or customer demand is binding.

rho.bridge: FDA documentation shows that regulated blood-establishment software, remote result validation, automated eligibility support, barcode workflows, and automated component separation are deployable, but it does not measure five-year conversion of the remaining exposed labor at independent centers. I translate demonstrated capability into implementation after haircuts for device clearance, process validation, standard operating procedures, cybersecurity, interoperability, capital, downtime, staff training, and the rule that trained users retain final clinical interpretation. Existing mature automation can make capability evidence overstate incremental implementation; labor scarcity and modular software could make the estimate conservative.
caveats: Device authorization and documented software functions do not measure realized labor conversion.
Existing automation is substantial, so gross technology capability can overstate incremental opportunity.
FDA validation, traceability, quality, and cybersecurity requirements slow deployment and preserve accountable human review.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent US blood and plasma centers reporting software and equipment commissioning, validation time, uptime, labor hours by workflow, error and discard rates, quality events, cybersecurity downtime, and post-install staffing would materially change the value.
rho.rationale: The industry has a credible installed technology base on which AI-assisted scheduling, donor communication, document review, forecasting, quality analytics, and exception routing can build. Implementation remains constrained by validated workflows and full traceability across collection, processing, compatibility testing, storage, and distribution, and FDA examples preserve trained-user judgment for clinical interpretation.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sell-or-transfer intentions among all US employer-business owners, and Healthcare Dive reports broad health-services deal activity rather than completed control transfers of eligible blood and plasma operators. I set the central probability well below Gallup's 22% intention rate because intentions fail to close, nonprofit affiliations and chain ownership are common, and FDA registrations, validated processes, donor networks, and customer approvals narrow the buyer pool. Broad healthcare deal value and demand for scalable operations may make a very low rate conservative, while general-owner intentions bias completed qualifying transfers upward.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions rather than completed transfers and covers all employer businesses.
Broad health-services M&A data do not identify blood or plasma operators or provide an eligible-firm denominator.
Nonprofit governance, chain ownership, licensing, and quality history can prevent an apparent establishment from being a transferable control target.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction cohort linking FDA-registered blood and plasma establishments to eligible-firm ownership, announced and failed sales, control status, nonprofit affiliations, regulatory observations, buyer type, and post-close license continuity would materially change the value.
s5.rationale: Succession pressure exists across employer businesses, and health-services buyers remain active but selective. For blood and plasma operators, transferability depends on regulatory standing, quality history, donor recruitment, facility approvals, customer concentration, and whether the business is truly independent rather than a site within a larger system.

principal_driver: AI can compress standardized donor, quality, inventory, and logistics work around a physical service that hospitals and manufacturers still need performed by a regulated operator.
principal_weakness: The coherent independent target pool is hard to observe and likely constrained by nonprofit systems, large chains, existing automation, regulatory validation, and donor or customer concentration.
